content.problemStatement:
In C programming, structures and unions are used to group data. Structures allocate memory for each member, while unions share the same memory location for all members.

Write a C program to compare the memory size of a structure and a union that contain the same members: an integer `roll` and a float `marks`. Print the size of both to demonstrate that a union occupies memory equal to its largest member.


Input Format

No input is required for this problem.


Output Format

The output should consist of two lines:

`Size of structure: <size_of_structure>`

`Size of union: <size_of_union>`


where `<size_of_structure>` and `<size_of_union>` are the sizes (in bytes) of the structure and the union, respectively.
content.outputFormat:
Two lines:
Size of structure: <size_of_structure>
Size of union: <size_of_union>
where <size_of_structure> and <size_of_union> are the sizes in bytes.

sampleTestCases.explanation: On a typical system int and float are 4 bytes each. The struct stores both members, giving 8 bytes. The union shares memory, so its size equals the largest member, 4 bytes.
Same as sample 1; there is no input, so the program always prints the same sizes.
Repeated demonstration that the output is independent of any input.

solution.approach: Define a struct with an int and a float and a union with the same members. Use the sizeof operator to obtain the memory occupied by each type. Print the results. No computation or input handling is needed.
